660271.name: Shohei Ohtani
660271.background: The most extraordinary player in baseball history, Ohtani is the only active two-way star who functions as a legitimate ace starter and middle-of-the-order slugger simultaneously. He won back-to-back AL MVP awards in 2021 and 2023 while pitching for the Angels, posted a 50 HR / 59 SB season in 2024 (hitting-only while recovering from Tommy John surgery), and signed a staggering 10-year/$700M contract with the Dodgers. In CBA scoring, his dual contribution is unmatched — he generates elite hitter points (HR=5, R+RBI stacking) plus SP point totals (IP×3, QS+3, K+1), making him the clear #1 keeper in any format.
660271.recentAnalysis: Through 18 games at the plate (.254/.402/.508, 5 HR, 10 RBI, 14 BB in 82 PA) and 3 starts on the mound (18.0 IP, 0.50 ERA, 0.72 WHIP, 18 K, 2 W), Ohtani is putting up a historic two-way campaign — his pitching line in particular is among the best in baseball through April.

592450.name: Aaron Judge
592450.background: A two-time AL MVP (2022, 2024) and the reigning AL HR record holder (62 in 2022), Judge re-signed with the Yankees on a 9-year/$360M deal and has been one of the most consistent CBA producers available — his 2022 and 2024 campaigns each generated elite CBA totals well above 600 points thanks to his combination of power (each HR = 5 pts), high RBI/R totals, and improving walk rate that suppresses K damage. Hamstring concerns limited him in 2023 but he's durable enough for keeper consideration at the top of any draft.
592450.recentAnalysis: Judge is off to a powerful start in 2026, going .243/.333/.608 with 8 HR, 14 RBI, and 16 runs in 20 games — his .608 SLG and pace of 65+ HR over a full season signal he's locked in, with the early low OBP likely to normalize upward as the season progresses.

701762.name: Nick Kurtz
701762.background: The Oakland Athletics' 2024 #1 overall draft pick out of Wake Forest, Kurtz is a left-handed 1B with massive raw power and an advanced understanding of the strike zone for his age — his elite walk rates at every minor-league stop translated immediately to the big league. His EROSP of 593 reflects a ceiling comparable to the best 1B in the game if his contact rate improves; as a potential dynasty keeper in CBA at 21 years old, he offers the highest long-term upside of any first baseman in the pool.
701762.recentAnalysis: Kurtz is showing his patient approach in his young MLB career (.206/.383/.302, 1 HR, 18 BB vs 30 K in 81 PA) — the walk rate is exceptional but the power hasn't ignited yet and the swing-and-miss rate is a concern to monitor as pitchers continue to attack him with breaking balls.

669373.name: Tarik Skubal
669373.background: The 2024 AL Cy Young winner and reigning ace of the Tigers, Skubal posted one of the greatest single-season pitching performances in recent memory — elite ERA under 2.50, top-5 K rate, near-zero walk rate — while being arguably the best value CBA SP based on QS frequency (QS+3), Win probability (W+3), and consistent K accumulation. A left-hander with a devastating slider/changeup mix, Skubal has emerged as a true franchise pitcher worth protecting at nearly any keeper cost.
669373.recentAnalysis: Skubal is picking up right where 2024 left off: 4 starts, 24.1 IP, 2.22 ERA, 0.95 WHIP, 23 K, only 4 BB — the command and strikeout rate are both elite, though he's yet to collect a quality start by the technical definition despite the excellent ERA.

682998.name: Corbin Carroll
682998.background: The 2023 NL Rookie of the Year for Arizona, Carroll burst onto the scene with 25 HR and 54 SB — a speed-power combo that makes CBA managers salivate given the +2/−1 stolen base scoring. A knee injury derailed his 2024 season and his power regressed significantly, but his athleticism and age (24) give him enormous bounce-back upside; he's a cornerstone keeper type when healthy, and his outfield eligibility makes him slot-flexible.
682998.recentAnalysis: Carroll is making a strong statement through the first three weeks of 2026, batting .311/.394/1.001 OPS with 2 HR, 3 triples, 6 doubles, 12 runs, and 11 RBI in 17 games — the elite slash line and extra-base hit pace suggest his full toolkit is back.

660670.name: Ronald Acuna Jr.
660670.background: The 2023 NL MVP who became the first player ever to post a 40 HR / 70 SB season, Acuna is the prototype elite fantasy player in CBA scoring given the SB+2 bonus and his power potential. He tore his ACL in June 2024, costing him most of that season, and spent 2025 working back to full form. When healthy he's the most dynamic player in baseball — a career 30+ SB pace with 30+ HR ceiling — and remains a first-round keeper asset.
660670.recentAnalysis: Acuna has shown encouraging speed (4 SB in 20 games) as he ramps back up post-ACL, but the power has been slow to materialize (.247/.344/.377, 1 HR, OPS .721) — historically his production surges once he finds his rhythm mid-season.

663656.name: Kyle Tucker
663656.background: A perennial 30 HR / 20+ SB threat who spent years quietly being one of the most underrated fantasy players in baseball while with Houston, Tucker was traded to the Dodgers and elevated into their star-studded lineup. His CBA value is elite — the power-speed combination scores heavily in the H+TB, SB+2 system, and his consistent contact minimizes K damage. He's a proven keeper asset with multiple 700+ CBA point seasons to his credit.
663656.recentAnalysis: Tucker is off to a modest start in Los Angeles (.239/.350/.343, 2 HR, 11 RBI, 3 SB in 18 games) — the power has been suppressed relative to his career norms but the walk rate remains excellent, and the lineup protection he receives batting behind Ohtani and others should help his counting stats.

676979.name: Garrett Crochet
676979.background: After years as an elite reliever for the White Sox, Crochet successfully transitioned to the rotation in 2024 and was traded to the Red Sox in a blockbuster deal. His electric fastball/slider combination gives him elite strikeout upside that pays dividends in CBA scoring (K+1 per K), and his ceiling as a full-season starter with Fenway home games makes him a top-10 keeper SP. The durability questions from his transition are the primary risk.
676979.recentAnalysis: Crochet has been roughed up to start 2026 (4 GS, 19.0 IP, 7.58 ERA, 1.58 WHIP, 7 BB) — the strikeout rate remains strong (22 K) but hard contact and elevated walk rate are clear concerns, suggesting potential mechanical or sequencing issues early in the season.

665742.name: Juan Soto
665742.background: One of the most disciplined hitters alive, Soto signed a record-shattering 15-year/$765M contract with the Mets after his 2024 season with the Yankees. His combination of elite OBP (career .420+), growing power (40+ HR upside), and near-perfect strike zone command makes him a consistent 700+ point CBA producer; the H+BB scoring rewards his approach heavily, and he only hurts you with the occasional K penalty. A generational keeper asset.
665742.recentAnalysis: Currently on the 10-day IL after a hot start (.355/.412/.516, 1 HR, 5 RBI in 8 games before going down), Soto was torching the ball before the injury — his IL stint is expected to be brief based on the 14-day remaining timeline, and his EROSP projection assumes a full return.

670541.name: Yordan Alvarez
670541.background: Houston's designated destroyer, Alvarez is arguably the most feared left-handed hitter in the game — a career .300 hitter with 30+ HR every healthy season, 2022 ALCS MVP, and remarkably low strikeout rate for a power hitter of his caliber. In CBA scoring, the H+1/TB+1 system rewards every extra-base hit heavily (HR = 5 pts), and Alvarez's ability to hit .300 with 35 HR makes him one of the safest high-floor producers in the pool. Injury history (oblique, knee) is the only real concern.
670541.recentAnalysis: Alvarez is absolutely destroying the ball to open 2026: .338/.489/1.224 OPS with 7 HR, 18 RBI, 18 BB, and only 10 K in 21 games — the contact rate and power are both elite, and at this pace he's on track for one of the best CBA seasons in league history.

686218.name: Emmet Sheehan
686218.background: A Dodgers starter who showed flashes of brilliance early in his career with a sharp fastball/slider combination, Sheehan has battled injuries including Tommy John surgery. His EROSP of 492 reflects his ceiling when healthy rather than his track record, as he hasn't yet logged a full healthy season at the major league level. In a deep CBA rotation, he offers high-upside SP production if he can stay on the mound.
686218.recentAnalysis: Sheehan has been hit hard in his 3 starts in 2026 (15.0 IP, 6.60 ERA, 1.47 WHIP, 11 ER, 6 BB) — while the K rate (14 in 15 IP) remains solid, the hard contact allowed raises concerns about whether his command or pitch quality has regressed from last year.

606466.name: Ketel Marte
606466.background: The heart of the Diamondbacks lineup, Marte is a switch-hitting 2B who posted a monster .332 average with 25 HR and 90 RBI during Arizona's 2023 World Series run. His multi-position eligibility (2B/OF in most leagues) and consistent ability to hit for average make him a reliable CBA contributor whose H+1/TB+1 scoring rewards his line-drive approach; he's been a 550-650 point annual producer in good health.
606466.recentAnalysis: Marte has started slowly in 2026 (.211/.273/.408, 4 HR, 9 RBI in 18 games) — the power is showing up (4 HR) but the batting average and OBP are well below his career norms, a likely early-season slump for a player historically known for getting hot in May.

608369.name: Corey Seager
608369.background: The 2023 World Series MVP and Rangers shortstop, Seager is one of the elite left-handed power-hitting shortstops in baseball — a career .280 hitter with 35+ HR upside who signed a 10-year/$325M deal with Texas. His fragility with the Dodgers (elbow surgeries, hand fractures) is the longstanding concern, but when healthy he's generated 600+ CBA point seasons. The Texas lineup context amplifies his counting stats potential.
608369.recentAnalysis: Seager is slugging through a slow contact start (.203 AVG) but showing his power pedigree with 5 HR and 12 BB in 18 games (.774 OPS) — the high K rate (20 in 78 PA) is slightly elevated but the power-walk combo gives CBA managers reason for patience.

807712.name: Luke Keaschall
807712.background: A Cardinals prospect who shot through their system on the strength of elite contact skills and athleticism, Keaschall is a young 2B whose EROSP projection reflects his upside in a lineup that should generate counting stats. His floor relies more on average and speed than power, limiting his ceiling in the H+TB CBA system compared to sluggers, but his youth and multi-position flexibility make him an interesting keeper candidate.
807712.recentAnalysis: Keaschall has posted modest numbers through 20 games (.219/.275/.288, 1 HR, 9 RBI, 3 SB) — the contact rate is acceptable but the power has yet to emerge, and his OPS of .563 suggests he's still adjusting to major league pitching in his first extended look.

691406.name: Junior Caminero
691406.background: Signed by the Rays at 16 out of the Dominican Republic and fast-tracked to the majors, Caminero is one of the youngest everyday players in the league with jaw-dropping raw power for his age — he showed flashes of 40+ HR upside in his early MLB appearances with exit velocities rivaling the game's elite. His youth means rough edges (K rate, plate discipline), but his ceiling in the CBA H+TB scoring system is enormous if the power plays full-time.
691406.recentAnalysis: Caminero has shown decent all-around production through 19 games (.250/.353/.444, 4 HR, 8 RBI, 12 BB) — the walk rate is an encouraging development for such a young hitter, and the 4 HR in 85 PA signal that the power is beginning to manifest against major league pitching.

695734.name: Daylen Lile
695734.background: A Cardinals outfield prospect selected in the first round of the 2022 draft out of Texas Tech, Lile is a left-handed hitter known for an advanced approach and solid contact skills in the minors. He doesn't profile as a big power threat, which limits his ceiling in the CBA HR-heavy scoring system, but his ability to accumulate hits (.282+ averages) and solid run production keep him in the counting stats race for CBA managers seeking high-floor starters.
695734.recentAnalysis: Lile has been making consistent contact in his early MLB action (.282/.322/.376, 1 HR, 8 RBI, 13 R in 20 games) — the SLG is modest and the SB pace is concerning given 2 CS vs 1 SB, but his contact-first approach keeps him producing across the CBA point categories.

672695.name: Geraldo Perdomo
672695.background: The Diamondbacks starting shortstop, Perdomo is defined by elite plate discipline (career 12%+ walk rate, sub-10% K rate) and plus speed, making him a strong base for SB accumulation in CBA's +2/−1 system. He doesn't offer much power, which caps his ceiling in the H+TB scoring, but his speed, walks, and low K rate minimize negative point production — he's a steady contributor who punishes you less than he rewards you in the right slots.
672695.recentAnalysis: Perdomo is already demonstrating his calling card through 19 games — 6 SB with only 3 CS, 11 BB against just 7 K — but the .194 average and .581 OPS show that the overall offensive value is limited; his CBA value is almost entirely tied to the SB accumulation.

691016.name: Tyler Soderstrom
691016.background: The Athletics' 2021 first-round pick was a catcher in the minors who transitioned to the outfield/DH role to protect his bat, and he's shown legitimate big-league hitting ability since his debut. His patient approach and gap power give him a solid floor for CBA point accumulation (14 RBI already through 19 games reflects good lineup placement), and his age-22/23 development arc makes him a keeper candidate if the Athletics are in his long-term plans.
691016.recentAnalysis: Soderstrom is posting solid RBI production (.211/.309/.394, 2 HR, 14 RBI in 19 games) with a decent walk rate — the .211 batting average and modest OPS are concerning but the RBI total shows he's driving in runners when given the opportunity.

808967.name: Yoshinobu Yamamoto
808967.background: Signed to a historic 12-year/$325M contract by the Dodgers, Yamamoto came to MLB as a three-time NPB Sawamura Award winner (the Japanese Cy Young) with arguably the best arsenal of any pitcher on the planet — devastating splitter, elite fastball command, and advanced pitching IQ. He missed most of 2024 with elbow surgery but was returning; in the CBA pitcher scoring system, his quality start frequency, strikeout rate, and low walk rate make him potentially the top SP in the pool.
808967.recentAnalysis: Yamamoto looks absolutely dominant in his 2026 return: 4 GS, 25.2 IP, 2.10 ERA, 0.82 WHIP, 21 K, only 3 BB — if he stays healthy this is a Cy Young-level line, and his extreme ground-ball approach limits the hard contact that plagued him in his early MLB starts.

701350.name: Roman Anthony
701350.background: The Red Sox's consensus top prospect and son of former big leaguer Eric Anthony, Roman Anthony is a left-handed outfielder with a refined approach — advanced plate discipline, line-drive stroke, and solid athleticism. He was one of the most hyped prospects in baseball heading into his MLB debut and carries a high floor in CBA scoring thanks to his walk rate and ability to get on base; as a young keeper, his long-term upside is enormous.
701350.recentAnalysis: Anthony is showing his hallmark patience in his early MLB action (.235/.342/.353, 1 HR, 3 RBI, 10 BB in 79 PA) but the power has yet to show and the 21 K are a concern — this is typical rookie adjustment period and his EROSP projection assumes a significant power surge as he settles in.

693433.name: Bryan Woo
693433.background: A Mariners starter who showed breakout potential in 2024 with a 3.10 ERA and elite WHIP in his first extended big-league action, Woo is a contact-and-command pitcher who profiles as a mid-rotation stalwart with upside — his ability to suppress hits and walks minimizes the damage from non-K outs and makes him highly reliable for CBA QS accumulation. He's an excellent value SP whose EROSP puts him among the top arms in the league.
693433.recentAnalysis: Woo has carried his breakout form into 2026 beautifully: 4 GS, 25.0 IP, 2.16 ERA, 0.92 WHIP, 20 K, 5 BB — despite going 0-2 due to run support, his underlying numbers are excellent and he's generating substantial CBA value from IP and K accumulation.

663538.name: Nico Hoerner
663538.background: The Cubs' elite contact 2B, Hoerner consistently posts sub-10% strikeout rates that are historically rare in the modern game — he's a CBA-friendly bat because he collects hits, runs, and RBI without hurting you with strikeouts (K−1). His 2023-2025 seasons established him as one of the best contact hitters in baseball, and in 2025 he added surprising power and steals to his profile; his multi-year CBA point totals typically rank in the 500-600 range.
663538.recentAnalysis: Hoerner is having a spectacular start to 2026 — .342/.420/.553 with 3 HR, 20 RBI, 6 SB, and only 9 K in 88 PA — this is career-best pace across the board, and the added power and steals are translating into elite CBA value that would put him near the top of the league if sustained.

683002.name: Gunnar Henderson
683002.background: The 2023 AL Unanimous Rookie of the Year and cornerstone of the Orioles rebuild, Henderson offers rare power-speed production from the shortstop position — he hit 28 HR as a 22-year-old and has 40 HR upside as he matures. In CBA scoring his power translates directly (HR = 5 pts, plus R and RBI), and his SB ability adds to his floor; his one weakness is a strikeout rate that costs CBA points (K−1), but his raw production more than compensates.
683002.recentAnalysis: Henderson is in a familiar pattern to start 2026 — powerful but strikeout-prone (.202/.277/.476, 6 HR, 14 RBI, 27 K in 94 PA) — the power is real but the K rate is elevated and his OPS of .753 is below his true talent level; expect the average to rise as the season progresses.

605540.name: Brandon Woodruff
605540.background: Once the best pitcher in the NL outside of New York and Los Angeles, Woodruff was a perennial top-5 SP with elite strikeout rates and pinpoint command before a series of shoulder injuries erased his 2023 and 2024 seasons. He is returning in 2025-2026 as one of the highest-upside comeback stories in baseball — if the shoulder is truly healthy, his arsenal is historically elite and his CBA value (QS, K accumulation, W potential on the Brewers) rivals the top SPs in the pool.
605540.recentAnalysis: Woodruff has made a promising return through 3 starts (16.2 IP, 4.32 ERA, 1.08 WHIP, 16 K, 3 BB) — the strikeout rate is sharp and the command is solid, though the 8 earned runs suggest some rust or sequencing issues that veteran managers expect to work out quickly.

677594.name: Julio Rodriguez
677594.background: The 2022 AL Rookie of the Year and long-term face of the Mariners franchise, Rodriguez has tremendous raw tools — elite speed, plus arm, legitimate 30 HR power — but has had a frustrating development arc marked by inconsistent contact and a somewhat disappointing 2024 season after a monster 2023. He's signed to a long-term extension with Seattle and remains a top keeper asset given his age and ceiling; his SB game (40+ SB pace in 2023) is a major CBA asset.
677594.recentAnalysis: Rodriguez has had a slow start to 2026 (.200/.310/.267, 1 HR, 6 RBI, 23 K in 87 PA) — the power and speed have both been absent through April and the 23 K is costly in CBA scoring; his EROSP projection anticipates a strong second-half resurgence consistent with his career patterns.

805300.name: Jakob Marsee
805300.background: An Athletics outfield prospect known for elite sprint speed and outstanding outfield defense, Marsee is one of the fastest players in baseball — his ability to steal bases (CBA: SB+2) is the primary driver of his EROSP projection. His bat has been raw in the minors and early MLB stints, with contact and power both lagging behind his athleticism, but his speed and on-base approach give him a distinct CBA niche as a stolen base specialist in a lineup that can leverage his traits.
805300.recentAnalysis: Marsee is struggling at the plate (.156/.275/.221) through 20 games but has accumulated 8 SB in just 20 games — that stolen base pace is elite and contributes substantially to CBA scoring (+2 per SB), making him valuable despite the low batting average.

701538.name: Jackson Merrill
701538.background: The Padres' young outfielder broke out in 2024 with surprising power numbers after being drafted as a contact-speed player, earning consideration for NL Rookie of the Year honors. His all-around game — solid average, emerging pop, and improving patience — makes him a well-rounded CBA contributor; the H+TB scoring rewards his extra-base hits, and his youth and development trajectory are appealing for a keeper-league manager.
701538.recentAnalysis: Merrill is off to a solid start in 2026 (.247/.301/.442, 3 HR, 13 RBI, 15 R in 20 games) with 6 doubles showing his gap power — the OBP is lower than ideal but the counting stats are legitimate.

647304.name: Josh Naylor
647304.background: A Blue Jays first baseman known for his fiery personality and solid contact-plus-power profile, Naylor is a left-handed hitter with legitimate 25+ HR potential who provides good RBI production when healthy. He overcame a horrific leg injury in 2021 and has been a reliable middle-of-the-order bat since, giving CBA managers a dependable 1B with reasonable R/RBI totals and solid average potential.
647304.recentAnalysis: Naylor has had a brutal start to 2026 (.141/.230/.218, 2 HR, 7 RBI, 4 R in 20 games) — the .141 average is dramatically below his career norms and the 4 GIDP suggest he's beating balls into the ground; expect significant positive regression.

682829.name: Elly De La Cruz
682829.background: One of the most electrifying young players in baseball, De La Cruz is a Reds shortstop with otherworldly tools — potential 30 HR / 60 SB upside that would generate massive CBA scoring given the SB+2 bonus and power premium. He launched 25+ HR in his first full season while stealing 37 bags, though his raw contact approach results in elite strikeout rates (K−1 in CBA). His ceiling is generational if the contact improves, making him a cornerstone keeper asset.
682829.recentAnalysis: De La Cruz is showing his enormous upside in 2026: .269/.352/.551 with 6 HR, 5 SB, 16 R in 20 games (.903 OPS) — the power and speed are both showing up simultaneously, though the 27 K will cost some CBA points across the season.

691718.name: Pete Crow-Armstrong
691718.background: The Cubs' highly touted centerfield prospect was drafted 9th overall in 2021 for his elite defensive profile and plus speed, with the bat considered raw but projectable. He's a true tools player whose defense in center is arguably the best in baseball, and his speed gives him 30+ SB upside that CBA values heavily. The power has been slow to develop and contact remains inconsistent, but his lineup spot in a strong Cubs lineup provides counting stats upside.
691718.recentAnalysis: Crow-Armstrong's 2026 has shown his strengths and limitations (.227/.275/.293, 1 HR, 4 SB in 19 games) — the speed is present but the 24 K in 81 PA is costly in CBA scoring and the power remains absent, suggesting he's still projecting rather than producing.

650911.name: Cristopher Sanchez
650911.background: The Phillies' quietly elite left-handed starter, Sanchez was one of the most underrated SPs in baseball in 2024 — posting a sub-3.00 ERA with exceptional contact suppression, a near-zero home run rate, and an arsenal built around his devastating changeup and plus command. In CBA scoring his clean innings (low ER, low H) maximize his IP×3 value; he's a high-floor workhorse who rarely hurts you, making him an excellent mid-rotation CBA keeper.
650911.recentAnalysis: Sanchez has been excellent through 4 starts (22.1 IP, 2.01 ERA, 31 K in 22.1 IP, 2 W) — the strikeout rate is elite and the ERA is Cy Young-worthy, though the high hit count (24) suggests some regression risk in his WHIP of 1.39.

607208.name: Trea Turner
607208.background: Philadelphia's leadoff shortstop who signed a massive 11-year/$300M extension, Turner is a career .298 hitter with consistent 30+ SB seasons and developing power — a CBA manager's dream in the H+SB scoring system. He had a down 2023 but bounced back as the Phillies reached multiple postseasons; his combination of contact, speed, and lineup protection makes him a reliable 500-600 point CBA producer when healthy.
607208.recentAnalysis: Turner has been hitting for contact (.260) but the speed hasn't shown up yet (1 SB in 19 games), which is the primary concern since his CBA value is heavily SB-dependent — historically his steals pick up as the season progresses.

621439.name: Byron Buxton
621439.background: The Twins' perpetually injured superstar is one of baseball's most frustrating fantasy players — when healthy he's a 5-tool weapon with legitimate 35 HR / 25 SB upside who generates enormous CBA value, but he's rarely healthy enough to deliver a full season. His career games-played rate is around 65%, and soft-tissue injuries (hamstrings, knees) strike every season. In keeper leagues the calculation is always: accept 18 weeks of elite production in exchange for 4-6 weeks on the IL.
621439.recentAnalysis: Buxton has been active and productive through 18 games (.236/.300/.431, 3 HR, 15 R) — the low SB pace is surprising for him, but the fact he's healthy through three weeks is itself an achievement worth noting.

641355.name: Cody Bellinger
641355.background: A former NL MVP (2019) who fell off a cliff before reinventing himself with the Cubs in 2023, Bellinger is a switch-hitting OF/1B with above-average tools across the board — not elite in any single CBA category but consistently productive across R/RBI/H/SB. His 2023 resurgence (.307, 26 HR, 18 SB) was followed by a solid 2024, and his multi-position eligibility adds lineup flexibility for CBA managers.
641355.recentAnalysis: Bellinger is posting modest but consistent numbers (.246/.341/.348, 1 HR, 6 RBI, 3 SB, only 12 K in 82 PA) — the extremely low strikeout rate (12) is his standout trait, minimizing K damage in CBA scoring, though the power has yet to arrive.

596019.name: Francisco Lindor
596019.background: The Mets' franchise shortstop is one of the most complete players in baseball — a .270+ hitter with 25-30 HR, 20+ SB, and gold-glove defense who signed a 10-year/$341M extension. In CBA scoring his combination of power, speed, and ability to hit in the heart of the lineup (R+RBI stacking) makes him a consistent 500-600 point producer. He plays a full 162 games virtually every year, providing the durability that amplifies his counting stats.
596019.recentAnalysis: Lindor has had an uncharacteristically slow start (.200/.297/.313, 1 HR, 1 RBI in 20 games) — the near-zero RBI total despite 12 runs suggests he's reaching base but not being driven in; historically he breaks out of cold stretches with multi-hit games and should rebound significantly.

700250.name: Ben Rice
700250.background: A Yankees first baseman who emerged as a starter after Anthony Rizzo's extended injury absence, Rice is a left-handed power hitter with an advanced approach for a young player — his walk rate is exceptional and he punishes fastballs. He's quickly established himself as one of the better young bats in the Yankees' lineup, and the Yankee Stadium short right-field porch amplifies his left-handed power in CBA's H+TB system.
700250.recentAnalysis: Rice has been one of the hottest hitters in baseball to start 2026: .339/.459/1.205 OPS with 6 HR, 16 RBI, 18 R, and 14 BB in just 74 PA — his elite combination of power and patience is generating elite CBA value and making a strong case for keeper consideration going forward.

657656.name: Ramon Laureano
657656.background: A journeyman outfielder who bounced between Oakland, San Diego, and elsewhere, Laureano is a solid all-around player known for his strong arm, above-average defense, and decent power-speed combination. He's never been a star but consistently contributes across multiple CBA categories — a reliable lineup filler who rarely hurts you while accumulating reasonable R/RBI/H totals.
657656.recentAnalysis: Laureano is having a strong start to 2026 (.290/.355/.551, 4 HR, 13 RBI, 3 SB in 18 games) — the .906 OPS represents elite performance well above his career norms, suggesting either lineup context or a hot streak that CBA managers should ride.

666018.name: Jonathan Aranda
666018.background: The Rays' patient left-handed 1B has one of the better walk rates in the league — his ability to reach base via the base-on-balls (BB+1 in CBA scoring) adds measurable value beyond his batting average. He's an emerging mid-rotation bat with solid contact skills and improving pop, making him a reliable contributor in Tampa's lineup and a solid bench piece in deeper CBA leagues.
666018.recentAnalysis: Aranda continues to showcase his exceptional patience (13 BB in 86 PA, .326 OBP) despite a modest .214 average — the 15 RBI reflect good situational hitting, and his walk rate keeps him contributing to CBA scoring even through batting average slumps.

668930.name: Brice Turang
668930.background: The Brewers' young second baseman has developed into a more complete player than scouts projected — his walk rate is excellent, his speed is a genuine weapon (30+ SB upside), and he's added surprising pop to his profile. In CBA scoring the combination of BB+1, SB+2, and solid hit accumulation makes him a well-rounded contributor; his career trajectory suggests a potential All-Star if the power development continues.
668930.recentAnalysis: Turang is off to an excellent start in 2026: .281/.410/.531, 3 HR, 11 RBI, 18 R, 6 SB, and 14 BB in only 17 games (.941 OPS) — the walk rate and stolen base pace are both elite, generating CBA value across multiple scoring categories simultaneously.

646240.name: Rafael Devers
646240.background: Boston's franchise DH/3B signed a monster 10-year/$313M extension and is one of the game's elite left-handed power hitters — a career .280 hitter with 35+ HR potential who has been worth 600+ CBA points in his peak seasons. He moved to DH full-time after defensive concerns, which actually helps his bat-only CBA value; his primary weakness is plate discipline (career sub-6% walk rate, 25%+ K rate), which limits his BB upside and costs K-1 points.
646240.recentAnalysis: Devers is off to an uncharacteristically slow start (.215/.262/.316, 2 HR, 7 RBI, 26 K in 84 PA) — the power has been absent and the 26 K are costly in CBA; historically he breaks out of these slumps with massive stretches of HR production, so managers should hold.

519242.name: Chris Sale
519242.background: A seven-time All-Star who won the 2024 NL Cy Young Award with the Braves after years of devastating injuries (Tommy John in 2020, multiple rib/shoulder setbacks), Sale has redefined himself as an elite strikeout pitcher and innings eater on the back half of his career. In CBA scoring his elite K rate (K+1), consistent quality start pace (QS+3), and win accumulation (W+3) make him a top-5 SP when healthy — the injury risk is real but his ceiling when active is among the best in baseball.
519242.recentAnalysis: Sale is continuing his remarkable late-career renaissance in 2026: 4 GS, 22.0 IP, 3.27 ERA, 22 K, and already 3 wins — the 3-1 record and solid peripherals suggest he's locked in as a reliable CBA SP ace through the season.

694671.name: Wyatt Langford
694671.background: The Rangers' 2024 draft pick out of Florida debuted that season with significant fanfare as a complete prospect — power, speed, advanced approach, and athleticism across all five tools. He's projected to be a franchise cornerstone and his EROSP reflects his ceiling in a Rangers lineup that provides excellent RBI opportunities. In a keeper league he has one of the highest long-term floors of any young outfielder.
694671.recentAnalysis: Langford is off to a modest start (.214/.247/.343, 1 HR, 4 RBI, 2 SB in 17 games) — the limited PA count and low OBP are the chief concerns for a manager who expected immediate impact, though his development arc suggests improvement as the season progresses.

694192.name: Jackson Chourio
694192.background: The Brewers signed Chourio to an unprecedented 8-year extension at age 19 before he even debuted, betting on his electric combination of speed, power, and athleticism. He has legitimate 30-30 potential in the medium term and is one of the youngest everyday players in the majors. In CBA scoring, his SB+2 upside and growing power profile (each HR = 5 pts) make him a future first-round keeper once he realizes his ceiling.
694192.recentAnalysis: Currently on the 10-day IL after showing some early 2026 production — with 14 days remaining on his IL stint, he should return within two weeks; his EROSP projection accounts for missed games and remains strong.

694497.name: Evan Carter
694497.background: Carter became a postseason legend for the Rangers in 2023, reaching base seemingly every at-bat during Texas's World Series run as a 21-year-old. His elite walk rate and on-base skills are the core of his profile — he rarely chases and works long counts, producing consistent BB+1 contributions in CBA. Had injury issues in 2024 but is establishing himself as a legitimate everyday OF with upside in the Rangers lineup.
694497.recentAnalysis: Carter is showing his patience through 20 games (.193/.324/.351, 2 HR, 3 SB, 11 BB) — the low average isn't ideal but the walk rate keeps his OBP viable, and the CBA scoring rewards his base-taking approach even when he's not getting hits.

656941.name: Kyle Schwarber
656941.background: One of baseball's premier three-true-outcomes sluggers, Schwarber annually hits 40+ HR with an elite walk rate and massive strikeout totals — the classic power-or-nothing profile. In CBA scoring his HR production is enormous (each = 5 pts), his BB+1 adds up, but the 30%+ K rate costs him in the K-1 penalty. In good seasons he's generated 600+ CBA points; the Phillies lineup provides excellent protection. A classic high-ceiling, moderate-floor keeper.
656941.recentAnalysis: Schwarber's 2026 follows his career pattern exactly: .209/.369/.522, 6 HR, 12 RBI, 13 BB, and 31 K in 84 PA (.891 OPS) — the power and walks are both elite and generating strong CBA value, while the 31 K are the expected tax on his approach.

608331.name: Max Fried
608331.background: After winning multiple World Series rings with Atlanta, Fried signed a massive free agent deal with the Yankees — giving him one of baseball's best run-support situations behind their lineup. A crafty left-hander with excellent curveball command, Fried is not a strikeout-first pitcher but generates outs efficiently and posts consistent quality starts (QS+3 in CBA) and wins (W+3 in Yankee Stadium). His durability and reliability make him a top-10 CBA keeper SP.
608331.recentAnalysis: Fried has been excellent in his Yankees debut: 5 GS leading all MLB starters with 33.1 IP, 2.97 ERA, elite 0.81 WHIP, 2 W — the contact suppression (only 19 H in 33 IP) is his best attribute and the Yankees offense is already driving wins for him.

676475.name: Alec Burleson
676475.background: A Cardinals 1B/OF with one of the best contact rates among young hitters, Burleson posts remarkably low strikeout rates (under 10%) that protect his CBA value by minimizing K-1 damage. He's a left-handed bat who accumulates hits and provides solid RBI production without needing to chase power, making him a reliable floor piece for CBA managers who value contact consistency over boom-or-bust profiles.
676475.recentAnalysis: Burleson is doing exactly what he does best through 19 games: .292/.388/.431, 2 HR, 14 RBI, and only 9 K in 85 PA (.819 OPS) — that K rate (10.6%) is elite for a power-capable hitter and he's driving in runs at a strong pace.

702332.name: Caleb Durbin
702332.background: A Yankees infield prospect called up to fill a gap at third base, Durbin is a contact-speed player without significant power whose EROSP projection reflects the upside of playing in New York's lineup rather than his current statistical track record. He's young and athletic, and the Yankees context gives him a higher counting-stats floor than his raw tools might suggest independently.
702332.recentAnalysis: Durbin has struggled badly in his extended MLB look (.138/.231/.190, 0 HR, 6 RBI in 65 PA) — the contact has not been there and the lack of power makes every at-bat crucial; he may be testing the patience of the Yankees and CBA managers alike.

667670.name: Brent Rooker
667670.background: One of the great fantasy breakout stories of recent years, Rooker was a career journeyman who emerged with Oakland as a legitimate power threat — posting 30+ HR seasons with a 40+ HR pace in 2024. His massive raw power is the engine (each HR = 5 CBA pts), and he's earned the right to be considered a legit keeper despite limited minor league hype. The A's lineup context limits his RBI opportunities somewhat.
667670.recentAnalysis: Rooker is currently on the 10-day IL after a slow start (.146/.245/.293, 2 HR, 8 RBI in 49 PA over 12 games) — the contact was down significantly before the IL stint, and managers should monitor whether the injury explains the slump or if there's an underlying issue.

680776.name: Jarren Duran
680776.background: After years as a contact-speed player who didn't hit enough, Duran broke out in 2024 as an All-Star with surprising power, posting 21 HR and 17 SB in an energetic Red Sox lineup. His combination of speed (SB+2), emerging power (HR=5), and leadoff-type run production makes him a compelling CBA contributor — the 2024 breakout elevated him from a dart throw to a legitimate keeper consideration.
680776.recentAnalysis: Duran is having a difficult follow-up through 16 games (.172/.254/.266, 1 HR, 3 SB in 71 PA) — his 2024 power surge has evaporated and the contact rate is alarmingly low; managers who kept him hoping for a repeat are watching a troubling regression pattern.

666182.name: Bo Bichette
666182.background: The Blue Jays' shortstop inherited his father Dante's name and athletic tools, posting .280+ averages with 20+ HR in his early seasons before injuries slowed him. His frenetic, hacking approach generates a lot of contact but also elevated strikeout rates — in CBA scoring he's a volume accumulator who collects H+R+RBI without elite results in any single category. A consistent 450-520 point CBA producer when healthy.
666182.recentAnalysis: Bichette is struggling through 20 games (.226/.278/.313, 1 HR, 9 RBI) — the power has been absent and the 0 SB eliminates a scoring category; his career averages suggest significant upward regression is expected.

672580.name: Maikel Garcia
672580.background: A Royals third baseman who emerged as an everyday player on the strength of his athleticism and contact skills, Garcia provides solid defensive value and a surprising amount of speed for the 3B position. His power has been developing gradually, and his lineup spot in a young, improving Kansas City offense gives him solid counting stats upside. A reliable mid-tier CBA contributor.
672580.recentAnalysis: Garcia has had a decent start (.269/.352/.399, 2 HR, 9 RBI, 12 R in 20 games) — the contact rate is solid with only 14 K in 88 PA and he's contributing across multiple CBA categories without being spectacular in any.

665862.name: Jazz Chisholm Jr.
665862.background: Traded from Miami to the Yankees, Chisholm is one of the most exciting players in baseball — an explosive 2B/OF with legitimate 30 HR / 30 SB potential that makes him a CBA dream in the power-speed scoring system. His aggressive baserunning (SB+2, CS-1) and plus power (HR=5) generate enormous ceiling production, but injuries and inconsistent contact have prevented him from reaching his full potential. When locked in for a full season he's a first-round CBA asset.
665862.recentAnalysis: Chisholm is showing his split personality to start 2026: 8 SB (elite CBA value) paired with a .174 average and 0 HR in 20 games — the stolen base pace is excellent but the lack of power is surprising and the contact issues need to resolve for him to deliver on his EROSP projection.

682928.name: CJ Abrams
682928.background: The Nationals' shortstop was one of baseball's fastest prospects whose power development turned him from a speed-only profile into a genuine all-around threat — he now has 25+ HR upside to pair with his 30+ SB potential, a combination that is lethal in CBA's SB+2/HR=5 scoring system. Signed a long-term extension with Washington and projects as one of the best values in the FA pool given how rarely he appears in CBA keeper discussions.
682928.recentAnalysis: Abrams has been one of the best hitters in baseball to start 2026: .364/1.151 OPS with 6 HR, 19 RBI, 12 R, and 4 SB in just 81 PA — this is MVP-caliber pace across every CBA scoring category simultaneously.

686469.name: Vinnie Pasquantino
686469.background: The Royals' patient first baseman has built his fantasy value on an elite walk rate and above-average power — his career BB% routinely exceeds 12%, generating consistent BB+1 CBA points even when his average dips. He's a left-handed bat with 25-30 HR upside who provides steady 1B production; the Royals' improving lineup context should amplify his counting stats as the team contends.
686469.recentAnalysis: Pasquantino has had a tough start (.173/.268/.281, 2 HR, 10 RBI in 86 PA) — the average is well below his career norms, though his RBI total shows he's driving in runners when given the chance; expect positive regression toward his career .260+ average.

543135.name: Nathan Eovaldi
543135.background: A veteran right-hander who overcame multiple Tommy John surgeries and elbow procedures to become a reliable mid-rotation SP, Eovaldi has reinvented himself several times over his career with a power fastball/splitter combination. With Texas he benefits from excellent run support (W+3 CBA) and his durable 180+ IP workloads generate consistent IP×3 value; his strikeout rate is a CBA bonus for a pitcher who doesn't maximize Ks.
543135.recentAnalysis: Eovaldi has struggled with hard contact early (4 GS, 21.2 IP, 5.40 ERA, 1.48 WHIP) but his 26 K and 2 wins show the underlying value — the Rangers offense is driving wins for him even in rough outings.

608324.name: Alex Bregman
608324.background: After a decade of excellence with Houston (two World Series rings, multiple All-Star appearances, 2022 ALCS MVP), Bregman signed with the Red Sox and brings his elite plate discipline and professional hitting approach to Fenway. A career .270 hitter with 25-30 HR and excellent BB rates, his CBA value is driven by consistent counting stats across R/RBI/H/BB; he's one of the most reliable 3B options and makes every lineup better.
608324.recentAnalysis: Bregman has had a slow start at Fenway (.241/.315/.329, 2 HR, 9 RBI in 19 games) — both the power and run production are below his career norms, and the 3 GIDP suggest hard contact isn't finding holes; historically he heats up significantly after April.

666160.name: Mickey Moniak
666160.background: The former #1 overall pick who struggled for years with the Phillies found his footing after a trade to the Angels (and then elsewhere), developing legitimate power in his mid-20s after years of unfulfilled potential. He's settled into a solid OF bat with 20+ HR upside who provides good at-bats when getting regular playing time — a quality depth piece who exceeds expectations given the weight of his draft pedigree.
666160.recentAnalysis: Moniak has been excellent in his limited 2026 action (.261/.300/.696, 5 HR, 9 RBI in just 50 PA) — the 5 HR in 14 games represents elite power pace and suggests he's locked in; if he maintains regular playing time his CBA production could be substantial.

624413.name: Pete Alonso
624413.background: "The Polar Bear" set the NL rookie HR record with 53 in 2019 and has been one of the most consistent power-first 1B in baseball since — annually delivering 35-40 HR that generate enormous CBA value (HR=5) and 100+ RBI. He departed the Mets in free agency after a long contract dispute and brings his elite contact-power profile wherever he lands. His 600+ CBA point ceiling in a good lineup makes him a perennial top-5 1B keeper.
624413.recentAnalysis: Alonso's power hasn't arrived yet through 20 games (.216/.363/.419, 2 HR, 8 RBI) but his elite walk rate (13 BB) is generating CBA value via BB+1 and the OBP suggests the hitting quality is there — expect a power surge in the coming weeks.

680977.name: Brendan Donovan
680977.background: A Cardinals utility man who became an everyday player on the strength of his exceptional contact skills and plate discipline, Donovan is a low-strikeout, high-OBP hitter who contributes across multiple positions — 2B/3B/OF eligibility amplifies his roster value. He's not a power threat or speed demon but accumulates CBA points through consistent H accumulation and walk rates, making him a reliable floor piece rather than a boom-or-bust option.
680977.recentAnalysis: Donovan is off to an excellent start (.304/.408/.547, 3 HR, 8 RBI in 18 games) — the unexpected power surge combined with his typical contact approach is generating elite CBA value well above expectations for someone primarily valued for his bat-to-ball skills.

547180.name: Bryce Harper
547180.background: The face of the Phillies and one of the most complete hitters in baseball, Harper won the NL MVP in 2021 while recovering from Tommy John surgery (batting only), then returned to peak form before battling additional health issues. A career .280 hitter with 35+ HR potential and an elite walk rate, his CBA value is enormous when healthy — the HR=5 system rewards his power, BB+1 captures his discipline, and the Phillies lineup provides elite RBI opportunities.
547180.recentAnalysis: Harper is having a strong early 2026 (.286/.413/.519, 4 HR, 11 RBI, 10 BB, only 11 K in 80 PA) — the combination of power and patience is both elite, and the 11 K in 80 PA shows his zone management is exceptional.

657277.name: Logan Webb
657277.background: The Giants' quiet ace built on an extreme groundball rate and masterful changeup to become one of baseball's best contact-suppression pitchers, regularly posting sub-3.00 ERAs despite pitching in a league full of strong lineups. He generates consistent quality start totals (QS+3 CBA) and innings volume (IP×3), and his durability (200+ IP three straight seasons) makes him a premier keeper SP despite not being a high-strikeout arm.
657277.recentAnalysis: Webb has been rougher than expected through 5 starts (30.0 IP, 5.40 ERA, 1.40 WHIP) — the Giants' defense has hurt him and his WHIP suggests a regression from his typically elite contact management; managers should expect improvement toward his career norms.

695578.name: James Wood
695578.background: The Nationals' massive outfield prospect (6'7", 240 lbs) signed a 11-year extension before even debuting, such was the confidence in his tools — elite raw power, surprising athleticism for his size, and improving plate discipline. He has a legitimate 35+ HR ceiling with developing speed and the Nationals are committing to his long-term development. In CBA his power potential (HR=5) and youth make him an excellent keeper building block.
695578.recentAnalysis: Wood has been impressive in his first extended MLB action: .253/.368/.556, 6 HR, 16 RBI, 17 R, 4 SB, 14 BB in 98 PA (.909 OPS) — the power, patience, and speed are all showing simultaneously, making a strong case for immediate keeper consideration.

681082.name: Bryson Stott
681082.background: The Phillies' second baseman provides steady contributions without a standout skill — decent contact, adequate speed, and solid defensive play at multiple infield positions. His CBA value comes from consistent at-bats in a powerful Phillies lineup that generates counting stats even for players who aren't stars; his SB pace adds a layer of value in the +2 scoring system.
681082.recentAnalysis: Stott has had a slow start (.204/.271/.222, 0 HR, 3 RBI, 4 SB in 59 PA) — the complete lack of power is concerning for a player whose modest ceiling depends on contributions across categories; the 4 SB are the lone positive in a difficult early sample.

681481.name: Kerry Carpenter
681481.background: A Tigers outfielder who emerged as a legitimate power threat despite being overlooked in the draft, Carpenter hits the ball extremely hard and has genuine 25+ HR potential. His swing-and-miss tendencies (25%+ K rate) cost him CBA points via K-1 but his power (HR=5) and developing approach give him a reasonable ceiling. The Tigers' improving lineup context should increase his counting stats opportunity.
681481.recentAnalysis: Carpenter is showing his power through 17 games (3 HR, 10 RBI in 62 PA) but the .192 average and 23 K reflect his swing-and-miss profile in full effect — the HR rate is the bright spot but the K cost is significant in CBA scoring.

686217.name: Sal Frelick
686217.background: A Brewers outfielder known for his excellent contact skills and above-average speed, Frelick profiles as a leadoff-type contributor whose CBA value comes from H accumulation and stolen base opportunities. He doesn't project for significant power, which limits his ceiling in the H+TB scoring system, but his consistent contact approach and BB generation keep his floor respectable in a Brewers lineup that gets on base.
686217.recentAnalysis: Frelick has struggled to find his 2024 form (.177/.301/.278, 1 HR, 3 RBI in 73 PA) — the average is well below his career norms but the 9 BB show the patient approach is intact; expect his contact rates to normalize toward his career .260+ range.

650333.name: Luis Arraez
650333.background: A three-time batting champion (won with Minnesota, Miami, and San Diego in consecutive seasons) whose extreme contact skills border on supernatural — he routinely posts .320+ averages with fewer than 20 strikeouts per season, an almost extinct profile in modern baseball. In CBA his H accumulation (H+1) generates consistent points and his near-zero K rate (K-1 penalty) means he almost never hurts your score; his ceiling is capped by complete lack of power and limited walk rates.
650333.recentAnalysis: Arraez is doing exactly what he always does: .319 average with 0 HR but only 4 K in 80 PA through 19 games — the strikeout rate is essentially zero and his contact-based CBA floor is exactly as advertised.

802415.name: Chandler Simpson
802415.background: One of the fastest players in baseball, Rays outfielder Chandler Simpson is essentially a pure speed asset — his stolen base production (SB+2 in CBA) is elite and his contact skills are solid enough to stay on the field. He doesn't project for power, which limits the H+TB component of his CBA scoring, but his SB production alone can justify a roster spot in leagues that value speed.
802415.recentAnalysis: Simpson is off to an excellent start: .342 average, 7 SB (tied for league leads), and only 5 K in 79 PA through 19 games — the stolen base pace is elite CBA value and the surprisingly high average suggests he might be more of an offensive asset than his pure-speed profile suggested.

687263.name: Zach Neto
687263.background: The Angels' shortstop has quickly established himself as one of the better young SS in the AL with solid all-around production — decent power, improving patience, and enough speed to contribute in the SB categories. He's playing through a rebuild with the Angels which limits his team context support, but his individual tools project for continued improvement as he enters his mid-20s.
687263.recentAnalysis: Neto is posting solid numbers through 21 games (.247/.360/.479, 5 HR, 18 R, 14 BB in 100 PA) — the walk rate and run total are both excellent and the 5 HR put him on a legitimate 35+ HR pace, making him a potential sleeper value.

677800.name: Wilyer Abreu
677800.background: A Red Sox outfielder with a promising bat-first profile, Abreu showed solid contact and emerging power in his initial MLB stints. He benefits from the Red Sox lineup context (high run environment, strong supporting cast) and his approach — good contact rate, manageable walk production — gives him a reasonable floor in CBA scoring.
677800.recentAnalysis: Abreu has had a strong start (.314/.373/.516, 3 HR, 10 RBI, 9 R in 75 PA) — the .889 OPS suggests he's hitting the ball well and the Red Sox lineup is amplifying his counting stats; a positive early trend for CBA managers.

666142.name: Cole Ragans
666142.background: A Royals left-hander who has emerged as one of the better young arms in the AL with a diverse arsenal and solid strikeout rates, Ragans has developed quickly into a frontline SP for a Royals team trending upward. His walk rate has been a concern at times but his stuff is legitimate — in CBA his K accumulation (K+1) and innings contribution make him a solid mid-rotation keeper option.
666142.recentAnalysis: Ragans has been unlucky through 4 starts (16.2 IP, 3.78 ERA, 16 K) but his 0-3 record despite the solid ERA reflects run support failures — the elevated BB rate (10) is the key concern, and the walk rate needs to improve for his underlying quality to translate to wins.

664285.name: Framber Valdez
664285.background: Houston's workhorse left-hander is defined by one of the most extreme ground-ball rates in modern baseball — his sinking fastball/curveball combination generates ground outs at a rate that suppresses HRs and extra-base hits, even in hitter-friendly environments. In CBA scoring his value comes from IP×3 and QS+3 rather than K accumulation; he's logged 200+ IP in multiple seasons and his durability makes him a reliable if unspectacular keeper SP.
664285.recentAnalysis: Valdez is pitching to his archetype: 4 GS, 24.0 IP, 3.75 ERA, 13 K, 1 W — the low K rate (13 in 24 IP) is typical for him, and the solid ERA reflects his ground-ball mastery suppressing damage despite a high hit count.

554430.name: Zack Wheeler
554430.background: The Phillies' ace is one of the most complete pitchers in the NL — multiple Cy Young runner-ups, consistently elite ERA, K rate, and innings totals, with a power fastball/slider combination that generates both strikeouts (K+1) and quality starts (QS+3). He's finished top-3 in Cy Young voting multiple times and been arguably the best pitcher in the NL not named deGrom in his era. A top-5 CBA SP keeper when healthy.
554430.recentAnalysis: Currently on the 15-day IL with 21 days remaining, Wheeler missed the early part of the 2026 season — his EROSP projection accounts for missed games and his return timeline has him back in early May.

680757.name: Steven Kwan
680757.background: The Guardians' leadoff OF has posted one of the lowest strikeout rates in baseball since his debut — routinely under 10% K rate — making him one of the safest CBA floor investments given the K-1 penalty structure. A career .290+ hitter with solid speed and adequate power, he generates consistent CBA contributions from H/R/BB without the volatility of power-dependent players. A perfect high-floor, moderate-ceiling keeper piece.
680757.recentAnalysis: Kwan is hitting at a slightly lower clip than usual (.253, down from career .291) but his defining trait — only 9 K in 89 PA — remains intact through 20 games; his limited K production minimizes CBA damage during the cold streak.

669302.name: Logan Gilbert
669302.background: The Mariners' ace has developed from a raw prospect into one of the AL's premier strikeout-and-command pitchers — his K rate is consistently among the best in the league, his walk rate is exceptional, and he's evolved into a legitimate top-3 SP on the strength of his curveball/fastball combination. In CBA scoring his K accumulation (K+1) is the primary value driver alongside consistent quality start potential.
669302.recentAnalysis: Gilbert is generating elite strikeout production despite bad luck with results (5 GS, 29.0 IP, 32 K, only 5 BB, 4.03 ERA, 1-3 record) — the 32 K in 29 IP is an elite rate and his command is excellent; the W-L record badly undersells his underlying performance.

681624.name: Andy Pages
681624.background: Andy Pages burst onto the MLB scene in 2024 as a Dodgers outfielder with plus raw power and an aggressive approach. The Cuban-born slugger showed 20-homer potential in his rookie season and earned a regular role in LA's loaded lineup. As a mid-round keeper option in CBA, his power ceiling and lineup support from the Dodgers machine make him one of the more intriguing upside bets heading into 2026.
681624.recentAnalysis: Pages is off to a scorching start in 2026, hitting .412/.453/.691 (1.144 OPS) with 5 HR, 21 RBI, and 3 SB across 19 games — one of the hottest bats in the early season.

621566.name: Matt Olson
621566.background: Matt Olson is one of the most reliable power-first first basemen in fantasy baseball, coming off back-to-back 30-plus homer seasons with Atlanta after being acquired in a blockbuster trade from Oakland. His elite walk rates and middle-of-the-order protection give him consistent RBI opportunities, and he remains a top-12 first baseman in CBA scoring year after year. Olson's kept status reflects his durability and high floor.
621566.recentAnalysis: Olson has a solid .299/.404/.599 line through 20 games with 5 HR, 15 RBI, and 16 runs, maintaining his trademark walk rate (12 BB) alongside good power production.

642547.name: Freddy Peralta
642547.background: Freddy Peralta is a strikeout machine for the Brewers who leans on a plus changeup and a power fastball to pile up Ks. He has posted multiple seasons with K/9 above 11 and, when healthy, provides a reliable mid-rotation floor with upside. His fantasy value is closely tied to Milwaukee's ability to win games, but the strikeout upside alone keeps him in the top tier of CBA rotation options.
642547.recentAnalysis: Peralta has been solid in his first 4 starts: 21.0 IP, 3.86 ERA, 1.14 WHIP, 25 strikeouts — the K rate is right on profile and the ratios look sustainable.

671732.name: Lawrence Butler
671732.background: Lawrence Butler emerged as one of the most exciting young power-speed threats in the American League while playing for the Athletics, combining 20-plus homer potential with double-digit stolen base upside. He showed the raw tools to be a five-category contributor in 2025, and at his age and price point he's one of the better long-term keeper investments in the CBA. His early-career strikeout rate is a concern, but the upside ceiling is loud.
671732.recentAnalysis: Butler has struggled early, hitting just .190/.250/.302 (0.552 OPS) with 2 HR across 19 games — a tough start with a high strikeout rate (18 K in 68 PA) dampening his fantasy value so far.

657746.name: Joe Ryan
657746.background: Joe Ryan has quietly developed into one of the better fantasy pitching values in baseball, relying on a high-spin four-seamer and strong command to generate weak contact and consistent strikeouts for the Twins. He owns a career ERA well under 4.00 and has shown the durability to provide a full 30-start season. In CBA, his combination of strikeouts, low walk rates, and W upside with Minnesota makes him a safe anchor for any rotation.
657746.recentAnalysis: Ryan has been outstanding to open 2026: 5 GS, 27.1 IP, 3.29 ERA, 0.88 WHIP, 28 K — his elite command is fully on display with a pristine walk rate and strong groundball results.

680694.name: Kyle Bradish
680694.background: Kyle Bradish was one of the breakout stories of 2023, posting a sub-3.00 ERA for Baltimore before Tommy John surgery cost him all of 2024 and most of 2025. When healthy, his sinking fastball and ability to generate weak contact made him one of the better deep sleepers in fantasy baseball. CBA managers who stashed him through the rehab are betting on a full return to form in 2026 — the ceiling is firmly top-20 SP.
680694.recentAnalysis: Bradish has had a rough return so far: 4 GS, 19.2 IP, 5.49 ERA, 1.63 WHIP — his control has been uncharacteristically shaky in these early outings after his extended absence.

671739.name: Michael Harris II
671739.background: Michael Harris II won NL Rookie of the Year in 2022 with an electric combination of speed, defense, and raw power for Atlanta, immediately cementing himself as a franchise cornerstone. His 2025 showed continued development in the power department, and at 23 he projects as a perennial 20-20 threat anchoring Atlanta's outfield for years. He's a prized keeper in CBA for any manager fortunate enough to hold his rights.
671739.recentAnalysis: Harris is off to a quiet start, hitting .258/.289/.410 with 3 HR and 2 SB across 18 games — the counting stats are building, but the low walk rate and modest OPS suggest he hasn't fully locked in yet.

673548.name: Seiya Suzuki
673548.background: Seiya Suzuki brought a polished bat and elite on-base skills from Japan's NPB when he joined the Cubs, and he has consistently delivered solid AVG and power from the right side of the plate. He profiles as a .270-.290 hitter with 20-homer upside and good walk rates, providing stable CBA value in the OF and UTIL slots. His durability has been a mild concern but he's a trustworthy mid-round keeper.
673548.recentAnalysis: Suzuki has only appeared in 7 games so far in 2026 (.286 AVG, 0 HR, 2 RBI) — he may have missed time early in the season, so his counting stats are just getting started.

663460.name: Kris Bubic
663460.background: Kris Bubic transformed himself from a struggling southpaw into a legitimate fantasy rotation starter with the Royals, posting one of the biggest ERA improvements in the AL in 2024 after overhauling his pitch mix. His ability to induce weak contact and generate groundballs makes him a solid depth arm who consistently eats innings for Kansas City's surprising playoff contender. In CBA, he represents the type of trustworthy mid-rotation piece that wins head-to-head weeks.
663460.recentAnalysis: Bubic has been solid in his first 4 starts: 22.2 IP, 3.97 ERA, 1.06 WHIP, 26 K — the strikeout rate is genuinely elite and he's keeping the walks under control.

682985.name: Riley Greene
682985.background: Riley Greene's development into a patient, high-contact outfielder for Detroit has made him one of the more underrated young hitters in the game, with a disciplined approach yielding 30-plus doubles and strong on-base numbers. He took a step forward in 2024 with improved power and a full season of health, and at 24 he's still climbing toward his offensive ceiling. In CBA, he's a solid OF option with upside in runs and OBP-adjacent categories.
682985.recentAnalysis: Greene has put together a decent start through 20 games: .254/.371/.355 with 1 HR, 12 RBI, 13 runs, and 10 walks — the walks are excellent but the power has been quiet so far.

605141.name: Mookie Betts
605141.background: Mookie Betts is one of the most complete and decorated players in baseball, a former MVP who provides elite contributions in every offensive category while winning Gold Gloves at shortstop after transitioning from the outfield. His Dodgers lineup protection is unmatched, and in a keeper league he represents a perennial top-15 overall value who has consistently delivered 25+ HR, 15+ SB, and a .290+ average. Multiple CBA championships have been built around his production.
605141.recentAnalysis: Betts is currently on the 10-day IL, having appeared in only 8 games (5 H, 2 HR, 7 RBI in 32 PA) before the injury — his managers are waiting for his return to an elite Dodgers lineup.

663556.name: Shane McClanahan
663556.background: Shane McClanahan was one of the most dominant left-handed starters in baseball before Tommy John surgery ended his 2024 season. The Rays ace had posted consecutive top-5 AL Cy Young finishes with elite strikeout rates, exceptional walk numbers, and a devastating changeup that completely neutralized left-handed hitters. A full recovery would return him to ace status, and CBA managers who kept him through the rehab are hoping 2026 is his coming-out party.
663556.recentAnalysis: McClanahan has made 3 starts in his return from TJ surgery: 13.2 IP, 3.95 ERA, 1.17 WHIP, 13 K — the stuff appears intact and he's building back up toward full workload.

669364.name: Xavier Edwards
669364.background: Xavier Edwards has developed into one of the better contact hitters in the NL, using a compact swing and elite bat-to-ball skills to post high batting averages with solid on-base rates for Miami. His lack of home run power limits his CBA ceiling, but his .340-plus contact rates, stolen base contributions, and exceptional strikeout avoidance make him a high-average catalyst who rewards managers willing to sacrifice the power category.
669364.recentAnalysis: Edwards has been one of the hottest hitters in early 2026, slashing .347/.424/.480 (0.910 OPS) with 1 HR, 16 runs, and only 9 strikeouts in 85 PA — his elite contact is fully on display.

670623.name: Isaac Paredes
670623.background: Isaac Paredes established himself as one of the better power-hitting third basemen in the AL during his time in Tampa, posting back-to-back 30-homer seasons with an extremely patient approach and a knack for hitting in high-leverage situations. After being traded to the Cubs, he brings a productive bat in the middle of Chicago's lineup and represents solid multi-position eligibility value. His CBA managers prize him for reliable HR and RBI output.
670623.recentAnalysis: Paredes has had a slow start in 2026: .200/.303/.286 (0.606 OPS) with 0 HR and 6 RBI through 16 games — the power has been completely absent but his walk rate is keeping the OBP respectable.

808982.name: Jung Hoo Lee
808982.background: Jung Hoo Lee arrived from the KBO as one of the most polished defensive outfielders in the world, and his first MLB season with San Francisco showcased elite contact skills and gap power that translated immediately. He missed significant time due to injury in his debut year but showed enough to earn multi-year keeper consideration. His five-tool ceiling — defense, speed, contact, gap power, and on-base ability — makes him a fascinating long-term asset in CBA.
808982.recentAnalysis: Lee has been solid through 20 games in 2026: .246/.333/.352 with 1 HR, 8 RBI, and a nice 7:11 walk-to-strikeout ratio — steady contribution across the board even without loud power numbers.

665161.name: Jeremy Pena
665161.background: Jeremy Pena won the World Series MVP in his rookie year, stunning the baseball world by replacing Carlos Correa seamlessly at shortstop for Houston and delivering in every clutch moment. He's developed into a solid all-around shortstop with 20-homer upside, excellent defense, and postseason pedigree. His CBA value is built on steady counting stats, multi-category contributions, and the constant run production that comes with batting in Houston's lineup.
665161.recentAnalysis: Pena is currently on the 10-day IL, having played just 10 games before the injury — he hit .256 with 0 HR in limited action and is awaiting his return to the Astros lineup.

650490.name: Yandy Diaz
650490.background: Yandy Diaz is the quintessential high-contact, high-OBP bat who has consistently outperformed his modest power profile by posting .300-plus batting averages and elite walk rates for Tampa Bay. He rarely strikes out, hits line drives to all fields, and profiles as a dependable .290 hitter who piles up runs batting in Tampa's patient lineup. In CBA his value is concentrated in AVG and OBP-adjacent categories, and his durability makes him a reliable floor play.
650490.recentAnalysis: Diaz is off to a terrific start: .365/.420/.587 (1.007 OPS) with 3 HR, 16 RBI through 19 games — he's making excellent contact as usual and the power numbers are running hot.

684007.name: Shota Imanaga
684007.background: Shota Imanaga arrived from Japan's NPB and immediately became one of the most effective starters in the NL, deploying a rare combination of deceptive arm action, an elite splitter, and pinpoint control to post ERA figures well below 3.00. His ability to generate weak contact and limit free passes makes him a fantasy ace despite modest velocity, and his Cubs tenure has been an unqualified success. In CBA he's become one of the most coveted rotation pieces in the league.
684007.recentAnalysis: Imanaga is simply dominant in 2026: 4 GS, 22.0 IP, 2.45 ERA, 0.77 WHIP, 31 K — a 12.7 K/9 with almost no walks is electric stuff and he looks even better than his 2024 debut.

678882.name: Ceddanne Rafaela
678882.background: Ceddanne Rafaela is one of the most exciting tooled-up players in the Red Sox organization, showing elite speed, plus defense in center field, and surprising pop for a player of his size. His early MLB exposure revealed raw offensive talent that was still developing, with speed and arm strength among the best tools on the Boston roster. In CBA he's a high-upside keeper with stolen base upside and a ceiling as a .270-hitting center fielder if his bat continues to mature.
678882.recentAnalysis: Rafaela has shown a solid early line: .283/.328/.406 with 1 HR, 5 RBI, and 9 runs through 18 games — a well-rounded contribution from a player still finding his footing at the MLB level.

607192.name: Tyler Glasnow
607192.background: Tyler Glasnow possesses one of the highest upsides of any pitcher in baseball when healthy, combining a 99-mph fastball with a 12-to-6 curveball that generates elite swing-and-miss rates. His injury history — Tommy John, oblique strains, forearm issues — has repeatedly interrupted what should have been multiple Cy Young campaigns, but every time he takes the mound he's a frontline fantasy ace. His move to the Dodgers put him in the best organizational system for health management and run support.
607192.recentAnalysis: Glasnow has been excellent in 2026: 4 GS, 25.0 IP, 3.24 ERA, 0.84 WHIP, 29 K — elite stuff on display with a 10.4 K/9 and the velocity looks right where it needs to be.

594798.name: Jacob deGrom
594798.background: Jacob deGrom is arguably the most talented pitcher of his generation — a two-time Cy Young winner whose 2021 season was so dominant it may never be replicated — whose career has been defined equally by brilliance and injury heartbreak. His mid-2020s seasons with Texas were shortened repeatedly by elbow and arm issues, but when he takes the mound his elite fastball-slider combination remains the most feared in baseball. In CBA, holding deGrom is always a high-risk, high-reward proposition.
594798.recentAnalysis: deGrom has been spectacular in his 2026 return: 4 GS, 19.2 IP, 2.29 ERA, 1.07 WHIP, 25 K — looking like the vintage ace when healthy, with pure dominance across the board.

672640.name: Otto Lopez
672640.background: Otto Lopez is a versatile, contact-oriented utility player who carved out a regular role with the Marlins through elite plate discipline and consistent line-drive hitting. His ability to play multiple infield and outfield positions gives him tremendous roster flexibility, and his batting average and on-base skills profile as a strong AVG contributor in fantasy. In CBA his speed, contact rate, and multi-position eligibility give him significant value beyond his modest power output.
672640.recentAnalysis: Lopez has been one of the early-season surprise performers: .347/.400/.573 (0.983 OPS) with 3 HR, 10 RBI, 14 runs, and 3 SB through 19 games — showing more pop than his previous profile suggested.

669432.name: Trevor Rogers
669432.background: Trevor Rogers flashed ace-caliber potential when he finished top-5 in NL Cy Young voting in 2021, but has spent the years since battling shoulder problems and mechanical inconsistency. When healthy and locked in, his mid-90s fastball and power breaking ball generate elite strikeout numbers and the ceiling remains that of a legitimate fantasy SP2. CBA managers have been patient waiting for him to recapture that 2021 form, and EROSP projections suggest it may be within reach.
669432.recentAnalysis: Rogers has been solid to open 2026: 4 GS, 23.2 IP, 3.04 ERA, 1.27 WHIP, 20 K — the control is a touch spotty but the ERA is excellent and he's flashing the swing-and-miss ability that made him a star.

592518.name: Manny Machado
592518.background: Manny Machado is a perennial All-Star and one of the best pure hitters in the National League, a disciplined third baseman who consistently delivers 30 home runs, 90 RBI, and a .280-plus average for San Diego. His elite walk rates, gap power, and postseason experience make him a cornerstone keeper in CBA, and he's never posted fewer than 25 home runs in a healthy season. He's been one of the most consistent fantasy assets in the CBA for multiple years.
592518.recentAnalysis: Machado has had a slow offensive start: .210/.368/.310 (0.702 OPS) with 2 HR and 10 RBI through 19 games — the walks are piling up (16 BB) but the power and batting average haven't arrived yet.

518692.name: Freddie Freeman
518692.background: Freddie Freeman is a future Hall of Famer and one of the most complete hitters in recent baseball history, a former NL MVP who has sustained elite production across multiple decades and organizations. His tenure with the Dodgers has been defined by postseason heroics — including a walk-off grand slam in the 2024 World Series — and continued .280-plus averages with 25-homer, 90-RBI production. He is among the most valuable and trusted keeper assets in the CBA.
518692.recentAnalysis: Freeman has put together a solid start: .282/.341/.487 (0.828 OPS) with 3 HR, 14 RBI, and 9 runs through 19 games — steady elite production from one of the most reliable bats in fantasy.

666176.name: Jo Adell
666176.background: Jo Adell was the Angels' most highly touted prospect in a generation, a five-tool outfielder whose raw tools drew comparisons to Mike Trout. After a rough MLB debut, he steadily improved his plate discipline and contact rate to become a legitimate starting outfielder with 20-homer upside and developing speed. In CBA, his athletic ceiling and power-speed combination have made him an intriguing long-term keeper even as he continues to mature into his potential.
666176.recentAnalysis: Adell has had a solid start to 2026: .302/.319/.451 with 3 HR, 14 RBI, and 11 runs through 21 games — he's putting the ball in play consistently and the counting stats are accumulating nicely.

624133.name: Ranger Suarez
624133.background: Ranger Suarez has blossomed into one of the Phillies' most reliable rotation options, leveraging excellent command and a heavy sinker to generate ground balls and eat innings in Philadelphia's powerful lineup. He's not a strikeout pitcher by nature, but his ability to keep runners off base and benefit from elite run support makes him a consistent W accumulator in fantasy. CBA managers appreciate his durability and steady ERA over the long fantasy season.
624133.recentAnalysis: Suarez has been solid in his first 4 starts: 22.1 IP, 3.22 ERA, 1.07 WHIP, 15 K — control is sharp and the groundball-heavy approach is generating results in the early going.

650402.name: Gleyber Torres
650402.background: Gleyber Torres arrived with enormous expectations as one of the Yankees' crown jewel prospects and delivered back-to-back 38-homer seasons before settling into a more modest but reliable production profile. After departing New York, he brings a switch-hitter's patience and positional flexibility as a second baseman who consistently draws walks and contributes across multiple categories. His CBA value lies in his durability, walks, and occasional power outbursts.
650402.recentAnalysis: Torres has been patient but unproductive early: .239/.384/.309 with 1 HR and 5 RBI across 19 games — he's drawing walks at an elite rate (14 BB) but the power and batting average haven't clicked yet.

671096.name: Andrew Abbott
671096.background: Andrew Abbott emerged as one of the better young arms in the Reds' rotation, combining a deceptive delivery with a plus curveball that generates big strikeout numbers against right-handed hitters. His ability to miss bats in a hitter-friendly ballpark in Cincinnati makes him a valuable fantasy commodity, and his development trajectory points toward a mid-rotation ace role. CBA managers are betting on continued growth from one of the NL Central's better young starters.
671096.recentAnalysis: Abbott has struggled in his first 5 starts: 20.1 IP, 5.75 ERA, 1.77 WHIP, 13 K — the command has been off and he's allowing too much hard contact in the early going.

664023.name: Ian Happ
664023.background: Ian Happ is a disciplined switch-hitter for the Cubs who consistently delivers 20-plus home runs, a solid walk rate, and legitimate multi-position eligibility across the outfield corners. His strikeout rate is his primary weakness, but his plate discipline and power upside have made him a trusted fantasy contributor for multiple years. In CBA he fills the power/OBP archetype well and provides positional flexibility.
664023.recentAnalysis: Happ is showing his usual profile: .214/.300/.500 (0.799 OPS) with 5 HR and 10 RBI in 17 games — the power is there but 27 strikeouts in 80 PA is an extremely concerning early pace.

666200.name: Jesus Luzardo
666200.background: Jesus Luzardo has one of the most electric arsenals in the National League, featuring a mid-90s fastball, a devastating slider, and a changeup that generates swing-and-miss rates among the best in baseball. His strikeout upside is genuinely ace-level, and his Marlins tenure has featured multiple double-digit strikeout outings. The knock on him has been durability and occasional command lapses, but when he's on, he's a fantasy difference-maker.
666200.recentAnalysis: Luzardo's 2026 start has been a tale of two lines: 30 K in 22.2 IP is absolutely electric, but a 7.94 ERA and 1.46 WHIP reveal serious run-prevention issues — the strikeout upside is undeniable but the ratios need to improve.

686948.name: Drake Baldwin
686948.background: Drake Baldwin emerged as one of the best young catchers in the NL, combining a strong arm, solid framing, and surprising offensive upside in a position typically light on fantasy production. His ability to hit for both average and power while providing catcher eligibility makes him an exceptionally valuable keeper in CBA, where C production is scarce. He's quickly established himself as a top-5 fantasy catcher and a cornerstone of Atlanta's lineup.
686948.recentAnalysis: Baldwin is off to a scorching start: .325/.374/.554 (0.927 OPS) with 5 HR, 19 RBI, and 20 runs through 20 games — exceptional production from the catcher position and one of the best all-around starts in the league.

645277.name: Ozzie Albies
645277.background: Ozzie Albies has been one of the most productive second basemen in fantasy baseball during Atlanta's dynasty run, combining above-average power for his position (25-plus HR) with excellent speed and strong run production from the top of a loaded lineup. His compact swing and quick bat generate consistent hard contact, and his durability over multiple seasons has made him a reliable keeper anchor. In CBA he's been a consistent first-round value at his position.
645277.recentAnalysis: Albies has posted a solid early line: .269/.310/.444 with 4 HR, 9 RBI, and 13 runs through 20 games — solid power contributions are there, though the speed game has been quiet so far.

656811.name: Ryan O'Hearn
